content.assessment: This proposal is unusual in explicitly targeting mechanisms distinct from the age-10 selection process: maintainer exemptions permitting Roma-only segregated schooling (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05) and geographic/settlement-driven stratification independent of academic tracking (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06). Institutional fit is mixed: it requires confronting a specific judicial precedent (the Curia's exemption ruling) and municipal-level zoning authority, both of which sit partly outside standard ministerial admissions control. By leaving the centralized entrance-exam mechanism (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03) and its associated 85% selective-school share (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01) fully unchanged, system coverage of the tracking-related sorting channel is intentionally zero, meaning this proposal alone cannot address the segregation dimension most closely tied to the exam-based selection statistics, even if it succeeds on its own distinct causal channel.
opportunities: Could reduce segregation through a genuinely separate causal channel without requiring resolution of the politically fraught track-age debate
strengths: Targets a documented, legally identifiable lever (maintainer exemptions) distinct from the admissions-exam mechanism
Recognizes geographic sorting as an independent institutional channel requiring separate governance tools
threats: Prior administrative desegregation measures already stagnated mid-decade, and the Curia's precedent creates a specific legal reversal risk for any exemption-review component
weaknesses: Leaves the centralized admissions/selection mechanism and its associated high selectivity share completely unchanged
Legal and administrative levers (Curia precedent, municipal zoning under KLIK centralization) sit outside straightforward ministerial control
